cataloging_subject.label: The Lay Folks Mass Book and offices according to the Use of York (Simmons selection)
cataloging_subject.scope_note: The exact edition-level selection represented by University of Michigan CME item CME00052: Simmons's four parallel Lay Folks' Mass Book texts B, C, E, and F with selected readings from A and D; Bidding Prayers II-V; the York Hours of the Cross; Appendices III-V; and selected notes and fragments. The record does not collapse these independent medieval works and versions, their manuscript witnesses, Simmons's full 1879 volume, the U-M encoding, or the repository XML into one abstract medieval work.

catalog_summary.title: The Lay Folks Mass Book and offices according to the Use of York (Simmons selection)
catalog_summary.author: Multiple anonymous medieval authors and translators; John Lydgate attributed on selected components
catalog_summary.date: Medieval components attested from the late fourteenth through early sixteenth centuries; source edition published 1879
catalog_summary.region: England, especially York and northern England; LFMB transmission also associated with Derbyshire/Nottinghamshire and Norfolk

titles.value: The Lay Folks Mass Book and offices according to the Use of York (Simmons selection)
notes: The qualifier signals that CME00052 is an edition-level selection and avoids implying that every component is part of the medieval LFMB poem.

agents.name: Anonymous translator of the Lay Folks' Mass Book
notes: The poem names 'dam Jeremy' in its account of a source, but neither that figure nor a proposed French exemplar has been securely identified.

agents.name: John Lydgate
notes: Attributed author of Appendix V, Merita Missae, and of Virtutes Missarum quoted in the encoded notes.

agents.name: Thomas Frederick Simmons
notes: Editor, compiler, collator, and English prose annotator of the 1879 source volume.

scope.description: Independent anonymous texts and prayers

assertion.notes: The existence of a translation is supported by the poem's own account and editorial analysis; the source and translator's identity remain unresolved.

scope.description: Merita Missae and the opening of Virtutes Missarum

notes: The scope excludes unrelated note fragments and does not assign Appendix IV to Lydgate merely because the current Bodleian IIIF range supplies that attribution as provider metadata.

date_statements.display: The source edition was published in 1879
notes: This dates the edited selection, not the medieval components.

date_statements.display: LFMB is first known from the late-fourteenth-century Royal MS 17 B XVII; modern scholarship finds no evidence for English composition much earlier
notes: This is first-known-attestation evidence, not a firm composition range and not support for Simmons's speculative early French-source theory.

date_statements.display: The encoded medieval components are chiefly witnessed in manuscripts of the late fourteenth and fifteenth centuries; Bidding Prayer V is taken from a 1509 print

notes: The range must not be interpreted as one work's composition period.

subject_statements.label: Intercession for the living and the dead

scope.description: Four parallel versions plus variant readings and later collateral witnesses

summaries.value: A selective electronic edition of medieval English materials for hearing and understanding Mass. Its core is four distinct manuscript versions of the verse Lay Folks' Mass Book, presented with variant readings. It also includes York bidding prayers for communal intercession, a bilingual York Hours of the Cross, prose preparation for Communion, two verse guides to hearing Mass, Lydgate-attributed material, and comparative fragments. Simmons's modern arrangement, glosses, translations, and apparatus remain integral to the object represented here.

CME00052 omits substantial runs of Simmons's volume, including most front matter, Appendix I, Appendix II, most notes, the glossary, and the index. Its medieval contents are neither one authorial work nor the contents of one manuscript.

extent.description: Ten cataloged content groups are represented by thirteen BODY-level DIV1 elements: a four-text list, the main LFMB parallel section, a detached comparison prayer from F, four bidding-prayer divisions, a York Hours part title and text, three appendices, and selected notes/fragments. The BODY has 304 P, 104 LG, 3,172 L, 765 NOTE, 181 PB, and 994 MILESTONE elements. These are encoding metrics, not canonical medieval-work counts; PBs repeat across parallel versions.

content_parts.label: The Lay Folks' Mass Book in four texts
content_parts.summary: Four parallel and materially distinct LFMB versions B, C, E, and F, with selected A and D readings and a detached F prayer. The verse instructs an audience how to attend, pray, and behave through the Mass.
notes: A and D are variant-reading sources, not fifth and sixth parallel streams. G-I are later-discovered collateral versions and are absent from Simmons.

content_parts.label: Bidding Prayer II
content_parts.summary: A Middle English and Latin York communal intercession for the Church, realm, parish, living, sick, travelers, women in childbirth, debtors, prisoners, benefactors, and dead.

content_parts.label: Bidding Prayer III
content_parts.summary: A longer Middle English and Latin bidding prayer from the York Minster Fothergill Manual.

content_parts.label: Bidding Prayer IV
content_parts.summary: A shorter later addition in the York Minster Manual, praying for the Church, kingdom, parish, travelers, sick, and dead.

content_parts.label: Bidding Prayer V
content_parts.summary: Preces pro diebus Dominicis from Wynkyn de Worde's 1509 York Manual, combining Middle English petitions and Latin psalms, suffrages, antiphons, and collects.

content_parts.label: York Hours of the Cross
content_parts.summary: A bilingual cycle from Matins through Compline meditating on Christ's Passion at the canonical hours. Simmons juxtaposes English verse and Latin text and supplies missing portions from other manuscript and printed sources.

locators.value: English fols. 1r-2v; Latin in discontiguous passages through fol. 24v

notes: The English Compline anthem comes from a Cambridge manuscript; the Recommendation comes from the 1543 Prymer; some Latin titles and bracketed words come from printed York Hours.

content_parts.label: Appendix III: Preparation before receiving the Sacrament
content_parts.summary: A Middle English prose devotional instruction on six considerations before receiving the Eucharist.
notes: Ashmole 1286 is the source of this appendix, not an LFMB witness.

content_parts.label: Appendix IV: A Treatise of the Manner and Mede of the Mass
content_parts.summary: A 688-line Middle English devotional poem explaining the benefits of hearing Mass and the actions and prayers appropriate during it.

content_parts.label: Appendix V: Merita Missae
content_parts.summary: A Middle English verse guide to daily devotion and hearing Mass, attributed by Simmons to John Lydgate.

content_parts.label: Selected notes and comparative fragments
content_parts.summary: A discontiguous extract from Simmons's notes, including the opening of Lydgate's Virtutes Missarum, comparisons with Lumby's edition of the Vertewis of the Mess, other manuscript comparisons, Latin quotations, and a final stanza from Harley MS 2251.

locators.value: Vertewis of the Mess, pp. 113-115, lines 3917-3993; cached PDF pp. 131-133

notes: This is not the complete notes section and should not be cataloged as one medieval work.

related_works.title: The Lay Folks' Mass Book tradition
notes: CME00052 contains four of nine known manuscript versions plus A and D readings; it is not a modern critical edition of all nine.

evidence.citation: General Corpus pinned CME source XML, CME00052.xml.
evidence.summary: Establishes exact titles, structures, forms, languages, page and folio milestones, editorial notes, and the ten content groups cataloged here.

evidence.citation: Structural inspection of General Corpus CME00052.xml.
evidence.summary: BODY contains 13 DIV1, 17 DIV2, 304 P, 104 LG, 3,172 L, 765 NOTE, 181 PB, and 994 MILESTONE elements. These counts support extent and part metrics without treating encoding units as canonical works.

evidence.citation: Thomas Frederick Simmons, ed., The Lay Folks Mass Book, EETS OS 71 (1879).
evidence.locator: editor's preface pp. ix-xvi; introduction pp. xvii-lxxi; encoded pp. 2-60, 64-80, 82-87, 122-154, 367-371; York Hours notes pp. 346-352
evidence.summary: Primary evidence for the edited-selection boundary, responsibilities, sources, titles, forms, contents, regional and dialect statements, witness loci, and nineteenth-century editorial arrangement.

evidence.citation: J. Rawson Lumby, ed., Ratis Raving, and Other Moral and Religious Pieces in Prose and Verse, EETS OS 43 (1870).
evidence.locator: manuscript statement at PDF pp. 9-12; Vertewis of the Mess at MS fols. 53r-54r, printed pp. 113-115, PDF pp. 131-133, lines 3917-3993
evidence.summary: Confirms the exact comparative source cited in Simmons's encoded notes, its title and boundaries, and its derivation from Cambridge University Library MS Kk.1.5.

evidence.citation: University of Michigan Library Digital Collections, CME00052 item information.
evidence.summary: Confirms U-M title, 1879 publication, Catholic liturgy and Mass subjects, public-domain statement, and content navigation.

evidence.citation: Jeremy J. Smith, 'The Manuscripts of the Middle English Lay Folks' Mass Book in Context,' Studia Anglica Posnaniensia 56(s1) (2021): 361-385.
evidence.summary: Provides the modern nine-version LFMB model, witness loci and dates, transmission regions, social contexts, and caution against an early composition date or conflation of manuscript and work.

evidence.citation: Combined official manuscript catalog and digital-object evidence documented in lineage:CME00052.
evidence.summary: Confirms current holdings, shelfmarks, physical descriptions, public-access status, and relevant part loci where cataloged. Exact evidence is separated source by source in the paired lineage manifest.

evidence.citation: Digital Bodleian, Vernon Manuscript, MS Eng. poet. a. 1.
evidence.locator: A Treatise of the Manner and Mede of the Mass at fols. 302v-303v, canvases 491-493
evidence.summary: Confirms exact Appendix IV boundaries and verse content inside the complete codex.

open_questions.question: What dates, places of composition, and responsibility statements can current specialist scholarship defend for each independent bidding prayer, Hours text, appendix, and note fragment?
next_steps: Consult component-specific critical editions and repertories rather than projecting witness dates onto composition.
Record competing attributions part by part, especially for Lydgate material and the York Hours supplements.

open_questions.question: What current shelfmark and exact folio ranges should be assigned to Bidding Prayers III-IV in the Fothergill York Manual?
next_steps: Request catalog reconciliation and a whole-codex reproduction from York Minster Library.
Map both prayers inside the delivered full object before upgrading the record.

notes: The preferred title is intentionally supplied and selection-qualified; the U-M and Simmons title forms remain separately searchable.
LFMB witness dates, dialects, and regional circulation are not silently converted into one composition date or place.
The G, H, and I LFMB versions are useful comparison sources but are not ancestors of Simmons 1879 or CME00052.
Manuscript-image completeness and rights are maintained in the paired lineage manifest, not inferred from work metadata.
